desc: Small bright green sphinx moth of coastal sage scrub. Day-flying — hovers at flowers. Green fades to yellow in preserved specimens. California near-endemic.

fm.Color: Bright green FW (fades in collection); dark HW

desc: Males gray with faint crossbands; females wingless. Larvae are common 'inchworms' on deciduous trees. Emerge after first fall rains.

fm.Color: ♂ gray with faint bands; ♀ wingless, gray-brown

desc: Small tan geometrid. Like fall cankerworm, ♀ is wingless. Larvae feed on oak and other hardwoods. Can outbreak and defoliate.
Small pale geometrid with fine dark speckling. Larvae on currants and gooseberries. Common at lights throughout LA suburbs.

fm.Color: Pale cream-yellow with fine brown speckling

desc: Small narrow moth that rests with wings wrapped around body like a tube. Larvae damage turf grass. The moth you flush from lawns when walking at dusk.

fm.Habitat: Turf grass, lawns — larvae damage roots

desc: Small olive-brown crambid with wavy pale crosslines. Larvae web together alfalfa and beet leaves. Very common at lights in agricultural areas.
Dull brown concealer moth found indoors. Larvae eat wool, fur, feathers, dried food, and even book bindings. Originally from Asia.
Variable brown noctuid with dark C-mark on forewing. Larvae are common garden cutworms. One of the most frequently photographed moths at LA blacklights.

fm.Color: Brown with dark C/dagger mark on FW

desc: Pale gray geometrid with wavy crosslines. Very common at lights. Larvae on many hardwoods. One of the 'LBMs' (little brown moths) that dominate LA blacklight sheets.

fm.Color: Pale gray with wavy dark crosslines

desc: Small bright yellow geometrid with brown FW border. Day-flying — frequents flowers. One of the few geometrids active by day. Larvae on honey locust.

fm.Color: Bright yellow with brown FW border

desc: Pink-and-yellow geometrid — strikingly colorful for a moth. Larvae feed on chickweed and clover. Common at lights. The 'cherry blossom moth.'

fm.Color: Pink bands on yellow ground — very colorful

desc: Large yellow-tan geometrid with pointed wing tips. Single dark line across both wings. Very common at lights. Larvae on many trees.

fm.Color: Yellow-tan; single dark diagonal crossline; pointed FW tips

desc: Black forewings with cream lines; orange-pink hindwings with black spots. Classic tiger moth pattern. Larvae ('woolly bears') feed on many low plants.

fm.Color: Black FW with cream lines; orange-pink HW with black spots
fm.Habitat: Grassland, chaparral — larvae on low plants

desc: Small tan crambid with dark markings. Larvae web together leaves of beans, beets, and many garden plants. Very common at lights.

fm.Color: Tan with dark brown spots and lines
fm.Habitat: Vegetable gardens — larvae web leaves together

desc: Tiny gold-tan moth. The classic fabric-eating clothes moth — larvae spin silken tubes in wool, fur, feathers. Weak flier that avoids light (unlike pantry moths).

fm.Habitat: Closets — larvae eat wool, silk, fur, feathers
fm.Behavior: Avoids light (unlike pantry moths); runs rather than flies

desc: Similar to webbing clothes moth but with three dark spots on each FW. Larvae build portable silk cases. Damages woolens, furs, and taxidermy.

fm.Color: Brown-tan with 3 dark FW spots (vs. plain in webbing moth)
fm.Habitat: Closets, museums — larvae in portable silk cases

desc: Pale green-white with brown patch and white dot. Larvae spectacular — green with yellow side stripe on oak. Classic prominent moth shape at rest (tented wings).

fm.Color: Pale green-white; brown FW patch with white dot

desc: Dark noctuid with pale crossband. Larvae are striking — velvety black with yellow lateral stripes. Pest of ornamentals and vegetables.

fm.Color: Dark brown with pale median band
fm.Habitat: Gardens — larvae velvety black with yellow stripes

desc: Cryptic bark-pattern forewings conceal brilliant orange-and-black hindwings — revealed in flash-and-vanish startle display. Larvae on oak. One of several CA Catocala species.

fm.Color: Bark-pattern FW; brilliant orange + black HW
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — cryptic at rest on bark
fm.Behavior: Flash-and-vanish: reveals bright HW when startled

desc: Tiny straw-colored moth that rests with wings wrapped tightly around body. Extremely common in lawns — the moth flushed by every footstep at dusk.

fm.Habitat: Turf grass — extremely common in lawns
fm.Behavior: Flushed en masse when walking through grass at dusk

desc: Spectacular wasp mimic with clear wings — one of the most convincing mimicry systems in insects. Larvae bore in sycamore. Day-flying. Often mistaken for a yellowjacket.

fm.Behavior: Day-flying wasp mimic; remarkably convincing

desc: Large heavy-bodied moth with mottled bark-pattern wings. Larvae bore 1-inch tunnels through living hardwood trunks. 3-year larval cycle. Can damage structural trees.

fm.Habitat: Hardwood trees — larvae bore large tunnels in trunk

desc: Dark mottled cutworm moth. Larvae feed on roots and stems of many garden plants. Very common at blacklights. One of the most frequently encountered noctuids in urban LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown mottled; pale reniform spot

desc: Pale tan-brown with single white dot on each FW. Larvae march in armies across fields (hence name). Major pest of turf and grain crops. Migratory from Mexico.

fm.Color: Pale tan-brown; single white dot on each FW
fm.Habitat: Turf, grain — larvae march in groups

desc: Small gray-brown noctuid with kidney-shaped (reniform) spot. Very common at lights. Larvae feed on clover, alfalfa, and many garden plants.
Small triangular moth with pointed snout (labial palps). Larvae are green 'clover worms' on legumes. Very common at lights — one of the first moths seen at any LA blacklight.

fm.Color: Brown; pointed snout; triangular at rest

desc: Small gray-brown litter moth with wavy crosslines. Larvae feed on dead leaves and decaying wood. Among the most numerically abundant moths at blacklights.

fm.Habitat: Woodland — larvae in leaf litter and dead wood

desc: Small dark brown owlet with slanted crosslines. Feeds on dead leaves and fungi on the forest floor. Common at lights in foothill oak woodland.
Small pale geometrid with sharp-angled crosslines. Very common at lights spring through fall. Larvae on many hardwoods. Subtle but frequent.

fm.Color: Pale tan with sharp dark crosslines at angle

desc: Delicate pale cream geometrid with scalloped brown wing borders. One of the most elegant small moths. Larvae on dandelion, chickweed. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Pale cream with scalloped brown borders — lacy appearance
fm.Habitat: Gardens, weedy areas — common at lights

desc: Small brown crambid with translucent wing patches. Larvae web leaves of beets, chard, spinach. Pantropical species — common in SoCal gardens.

fm.Color: Brown with translucent patches and dark spots

desc: Tiny gray-brown moth. Larvae mine potato tubers and tomato/eggplant leaves. Major stored-potato pest worldwide. Common in LA home gardens and warehouses.

fm.Color: Narrow gray-brown wings with dark speckling
fm.Habitat: Potato, tomato, eggplant — mines tubers and leaves

desc: Pale tan dart. Larvae cut grain roots underground. Serious western agriculture pest.
Tiny noctuid perfectly mimicking bird droppings — white-and-brown splotch. Remarkable camouflage on leaves.
Gray-brown dart with pinkish reniform spot. Common at blacklights. Cutworm larvae damage gardens.
Pale straw with dark vein dots. Larvae on grasses. Common at lights.
Striking white FW edged olive-brown and lilac. Resembles bird dropping or lichen. Larvae on grape.
Tan-brown with dark streaks. Larvae gregarious — black-yellow caterpillars arch when disturbed.
Small emerald-green geometrid. Larvae decorate themselves with flower petals — living camouflage.
Pale gray with sharp dark bent crossline. Very common at lights. Subtle but frequent.
Small olive-brown crambid. Larvae tie celery and beet leaves. Very common at lights year-round.
Small gray pyralid. Major flour mill pest worldwide. Larvae contaminate flour with silk.
Small gray pyralid. Major California dried fruit pest (raisins, dates, figs).
Small brown bell-shaped tortricid. Larvae roll leaves of grape, citrus, 100+ plants.
Variable tan tortricid. Larvae damage apple, cherry, citrus fruit and leaves.
Pale gray sphinx. Larvae on snowberry. Mountain chaparral. Clean elegant appearance.
Spectacular wasp mimic — metallic green FW, orange HW and legs. Larvae bore squash vine stems. Day-flying.
Small dark erebid. Larvae on sumac (Rhus). Very common at lights in chaparral.
Small white moth. Unlike true yucca moth, does not pollinate — cheater in the yucca-moth mutualism.
Tiny variable noctuid — one of the most abundant small moths at lights. Green, brown, or gray forms. Larvae on grasses.
Brown noctuid with woodgrain pattern. Spring species — among the first moths at lights each year. Larvae on many hardwoods.
Gray-brown dart. Larvae are cutworms. Common fall species at blacklights in LA.
Tiny moth with pink-and-olive pattern. Despite small size, beautifully marked. Larvae on grasses.

fm.Color: Pink and olive bands — colorful for tiny moth

desc: Small noctuid with distinctive pink median band across gray wings. Larvae on ragweed.
Pale whitish dart. Desert species common at eastern LA County blacklights. Larvae on low desert plants.
Rich russet-orange noctuid. Fall-flying. Larvae on oak. One of the more colorful noctuids.
Large brown noctuid with coppery hindwings. Cryptic bark pattern. Common at lights and bait traps fall.
Gray-brown with silver U mark on FW. Pest of lettuce, beans, and brassicas. Hovers at flowers at dusk.
Dark brown dart with pinkish tinge. Larvae damage vegetables and cotton underground. Year-round in SoCal.
Small gray-brown noctuid with subtle mottled pattern. Introduced from Europe. Larvae on low plants. Very common at lights.
Bright emerald green with white crosslines. One of several green geometrids. Larvae on oaks.
Large gray geometrid with bark-like pattern. Dramatic — one of the largest geometrids.
Males tan with dark crosslines; females wingless. Late fall species — emerges after first cold snap.
Olive-green noctuid with pink flush. Larvae exclusively on ferns. Beautiful subtle moth.
Famous 'industrial melanism' textbook moth. Pale with dark speckling. Classic evolutionary biology example.

fm.Color: White peppered with black — textbook evolution

desc: Gray with dark crosslines and bands. Very common at lights spring-fall. Variable pattern.
Tiny gray pug moth with four dark discal spots. Pugs are a huge genus — this is the commonest in LA.
Dark noctuid with kidney-shaped mark. Major worldwide pest of brassicas. Larvae bore into cabbage hearts.
Pale tan with dark spots and streaks. Spectacular hairy caterpillars on sycamore. Common in LA riparian areas.
Cream-tan with checkered pattern. Larvae spectacularly hairy. Common at lights summer-fall.
Gray with dark dagger marks. Larvae are green with red spots on oak. Fall species.
Distinctive scalloped hindwings. Desert/foothill noctuid. Larvae on low desert plants.
Gray with pointed hood-like thorax. Larvae spectacularly striped on asters and goldenrod.
White and gray with sharp dark crosslines. Attractive contrast pattern. Larvae on birch and alder.
Small pure white crambid. Very common at lights. Larvae in grass thatch.
Brown crambid with translucent patches. Very common at lights spring-fall. Larvae on grasses.
Pale straw crambid. Larvae damage carrot and parsley seed heads. Common in herb gardens.
Tiny brown-tan gelechiid. Larvae skeletonize leaves of many deciduous trees. Very common but tiny.
Tan-brown prominent. Gregarious caterpillars on walnut and hickory — arch heads and tails when disturbed.
Small dark moth with ruby-red hindwings. Woolly bear caterpillar. Flies early in season. Common.
Pure white with small black dots. Bright orange-yellow abdomen. Similar to Virginia tiger moth. Common at lights.

fm.Color: White with black dots; orange abdomen

desc: Pale with pink crosslines. Larvae on willow. Delicate and attractive. Common at riparian lights.
Pale with single sharp dark crossline from apex. Larvae on knotweed and docks. Elegant minimalist pattern.
Large bright yellow geometrid — looks like a dead leaf. Notched wing margins. Larvae on maple, oak. Fall.
Small gray pyralid. Larvae bore into legume seed pods — lima beans, soybeans. Cosmopolitan pest.
Tiny moth with white head and shoulders. Very common indoors. Larvae eat plant debris, dried food, fabrics.
Small furry moth. Larvae ('asps') are extremely venomous — covered with urticating hairs causing intense pain. Rare but medically important.

fm.Warning: Larvae extremely venomous — intense pain from hairs

desc: Emerald geometrid — bright green geometrid moth with delicate white crosslines. One of the most beautiful small moths. Fades to pale after death. Larvae on legumes.

fm.Color: Bright emerald green with white crosslines

desc: Pale litter moth — small gray-brown erebid of leaf litter habitats. Larvae on dead leaves and detritus. Very common at lights but easily overlooked.
Filbertworm moth — small tortricid whose larvae bore into acorns, filberts, and chestnuts. Important nut pest. Bell-shaped at rest with coppery wing markings.
Mexican tiger moth — black forewings with cream stripes; orange-red hindwings with black spots. Classic tiger moth pattern. Woolly bear caterpillars on low plants.

fm.Color: Black FW + cream stripes; orange-red HW + spots

desc: Six-banded choreutid — tiny metallic moth with six dark bands. Day-flying. Larvae mine in mullein leaves. Beautiful under magnification.

fm.Color: Metallic with six dark bands; day-flying

desc: Dark-eyed noctuid — small gray-brown noctuid with distinctive dark eye mark on forewing. Common at lights in chaparral habitat.
Lunate zale — large gray noctuid with scalloped wing margins and lunate (crescent) marks. Cryptic bark-pattern. Common at lights and bait traps in oak woodland.

fm.Color: Gray with scalloped margins; crescent marks

desc: Ruddy dart — gray-brown noctuid with reddish tinge. Fall-flying. Common at blacklights in foothill areas.
Willow prominent — gray prominent moth with bark-like pattern. Larvae on willow. Tented wings at rest give distinctive profile.

fm.Color: Gray bark-pattern; tented wings at rest

desc: Green cutworm moth — pale green-gray noctuid. Larvae are cutworms on clover and alfalfa. Common at lights spring-fall.
Togated catocala — brown underwing moth with marked FW. Common at lights in oak woodland and chaparral. Larvae on various shrubs.
Coral bean moth — brown crambid with intricate pattern. Larvae bore into coral bean (Erythrina) seeds and stems. Common wherever coral trees grow.
Slender emerald — delicate pale green geometrid with faint crosslines. One of several emerald moths in LA County. Larvae on various trees.
Divergent underwing — gray noctuid with orange-red hindwings. Flash-and-vanish display. Common in desert and foothill habitats.
Irrorated geometrid — pale gray geometrid peppered with dark scales. Very common at lights. Larvae on many shrubs.

fm.Color: Pale gray peppered with dark scales

desc: Half-marked oecophorid — small brown concealer moth introduced from Australia. Larvae feed on dead eucalyptus leaves. Very common wherever eucalyptus grows.
Dangerous crambid — brown crambid moth found in chaparral and woodland. Larvae web leaves of various shrubs. Common at lights.
Darker-spotted straw moth — pale straw noctuid with dark spots. Similar to corn earworm but smaller. Larvae on phlox and other flowers.
Straight-lined seed moth — tiny noctuid with straight dark crossline on pale wings. Larvae feed on Asteraceae seed heads. Very common at lights.
Ochre underwing — brown noctuid with ochre-yellow hindwings. Flash display in flight. Common in desert and foothill habitats. Flies to lights.
Coyote brush stem gall moth — tiny gelechiid whose larvae induce swollen stem galls on coyote brush (Baccharis). Very common in coastal sage scrub. Galls diagnostic.

fm.Color: Gray; stem galls on Baccharis diagnostic

desc: Edwards' glassy-wing moth — white tiger moth with translucent wings. Spectacular. Larvae ('woolly bears') on many low plants. Foothill and mountain species.

fm.Color: White with translucent patches; woolly bear larvae

desc: Belted grass-veneer — small crambid with distinctive dark belt across pale wings. Larvae in grass thatch. Common at lights in late summer.
Everlasting bud moth — tiny noctuid. One of the smallest noctuids — under ½ inch wingspan. Larvae on everlasting (Gnaphalium) flower heads.
Royal poinciana moth — brown-gray erebid with undulating crosslines. Desert and foothill species. Larvae on legumes including mesquite.
Vestal tiger moth — pure white tiger moth with black spots. Bright yellow-orange abdomen. Woolly bear caterpillars. Beautiful — one of the whitest moths.

fm.Color: Pure white with scattered black spots; orange abdomen

desc: Eight-barred moth — brown crambid with distinctive pattern of eight dark bands on translucent wings. Tropical species reaching SoCal. Larvae on legumes.
Planthopper parasite moth — bizarre tiny moth whose larvae are external parasites of planthoppers. Larvae feed on planthopper hemolymph. Extremely unusual biology for Lepidoptera.
Cotton tipworm moth — small brown tortricid. Larvae bore into cotton bolls, okra pods, and hollyhock buds. Common garden pest. Native to South America.
Buckwheat root borer moth — clearwing moth with transparent wings mimicking a small wasp. Larvae bore in buckwheat (Eriogonum) roots. Common in sage scrub. Day-flying.
Lantana plume moth — tiny plume moth with deeply divided feathery wings. Larvae bore in lantana flower heads. Introduced for biocontrol of invasive lantana.

fm.Color: Tan; feathery T-shaped wings; on lantana

desc: February moth — green noctuid with intricate dark markings. Named for early emergence in late winter/early spring. Larvae on conifers. One of the earliest-flying moths.

fm.Season: Late winter — one of earliest moths

desc: Edwards' geometrid — pale geometrid of chaparral and scrub. Larvae on buckwheat and other native shrubs. Common at lights in foothill areas.
Indistinct tussock moth — pale tan tiger moth with faint pattern. Spectacular hairy caterpillars. Common in foothill and mountain oak woodland.

fm.Color: Pale tan with faint darker spots

desc: Dusky raisin moth — small pyralid similar to raisin moth. Larvae in dried fruit. Important stored-product pest in California's raisin and fig industry.
Indomitable graphic moth — brown-gray erebid with complex line pattern. Common in desert and foothill habitats. Larvae on mesquite and acacia.
Western poplar clearwing — clearwing moth with transparent wings mimicking a wasp. Larvae bore in poplar, willow, and cottonwood trunks. Day-flying.

fm.Behavior: Day-flying; on poplar/willow

desc: Metalliferous moth — small crambid with subtle metallic sheen on wings. Larvae in stored products and detritus. Common at lights.
American sunflower moth — small pyralid whose larvae bore into sunflower seed heads. Can damage commercial sunflower crops. Common in LA wherever sunflowers grow.
Geranium plume moth — tiny plume moth with deeply divided feathery wings. Larvae bore into geranium flower buds. Common wherever geraniums are grown.
Manzanita ethmia — medium moth with spotted wings. Larvae on manzanita (Arctostaphylos). Common in chaparral. Attractive black-and-white wing pattern.
Historical moth — gray-brown erebid. Common at lights in oak woodland. Cryptic bark-pattern when resting on tree trunks.
Oak winter highflier — variable green-brown geometrid flying in winter. One of the few moths active in cold season. Larvae on oak.
Elder moth — brown-gray noctuid. Larvae feed on elderberry (Sambucus). Common at lights near riparian areas. Cryptic at rest.
Detritus moth — small brown moth. Larvae feed on dead plant material, paper, and dried foods. Found in stored products and greenhouses. Pantropical.
Pale graphic moth — gray moth with pale hindwings. Flash-and-vanish display — reveals hindwings in flight. Desert and foothill habitat.

fm.Color: Gray FW; pale HW in flight

desc: Pyralid moth with distinctive dark crosslines. Larvae on various plants. Common at lights in foothill habitats. Part of the diverse pyralid fauna.
Fragile geometrid — pale gray geometrid common at lights. Larvae on many trees. Delicate appearance. One of several pale Iridopsis in LA.
One-banded pyrausta — small orange crambid with single dark crossband. Day-flying. On mint family plants in sage scrub. Common and recognizable.

fm.Color: Orange with single dark band; day-flying

desc: Fractured western snout — unusual geometrid with snout-like labial palps. Brown with dark fracture-line pattern. Foothill and desert scrub.
Mountain-mahogany moth — spotted depressariid on mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus). White wings with black spots. Larvae on native chaparral shrubs.
Fortunate wave — tiny cream-colored wave moth. Very common at lights. One of the most abundant small geometrids in LA. Larvae on low plants.

fm.Color: Pale cream; very common at lights

desc: Powell's shield-bearer moth — tiny heliozelid moth whose larvae mine oak leaves. Larval cases cut from leaf tissue form distinctive shield-shaped cocoons.

fm.Color: Metallic; shield-shaped larval cases on oak leaves

desc: Opuntia leaf miner — tiny moth whose larvae create serpentine mines in prickly pear cactus pads. White winding trails on green cactus pads are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Pale; serpentine mines on cactus pads

desc: Dot-lined angle — pale geometrid with dotted crosslines. Desert and foothill species. Larvae on various shrubs.
Household casebearer — small moth whose larvae build portable silk cases decorated with debris. Cases found on walls and ceilings indoors. Larvae eat silk, wool, and detritus.

fm.Color: Gray; portable debris-covered cases on walls

desc: Hawaiian dancing moth — tiny tineoid moth. Larvae feed on dried plant material, lichen, and detritus on tree bark. Originally from Hawaii. Now widespread in SoCal.
Irene's underwing — gray bark-patterned forewing concealing bright hindwing. Flash display when startled. Larvae on willow. One of several California Catocala.

fm.Color: Gray bark FW; colored HW (flash display)

desc: Howland's graphic moth — gray-brown erebid of foothill and desert habitats. Larvae on shrubs. At lights.
Orange-winged Carpet. Geometrid moth. Larvae are loopers.
Five-spotted hawk moth — large gray sphinx moth with five orange spots on abdomen. Larvae are the green 'tomato hornworms' of garden lore. Important pollinator at dusk.
Brown-shaded mimorista — small crambid of desert margins. Larvae on various plants. At lights. Part of the diverse pyraloid moth community.
Lesser Cornstalk Borer Moth. Pyralid moth.
Glorious clearwing — spectacular clearwing moth with transparent wings and metallic blue-black body. Day-flying wasp mimic. Larvae bore in native shrub stems.

fm.Behavior: Day-flying wasp mimic

desc: Beam moth — small noctuid of chaparral and scrub habitats. Named for beam-like palp structure. Common at lights in foothill areas.
Pacific tent caterpillar — builds communal silk tent nests in trees. Larvae gregarious, feeding on oak and other hardwoods. Periodic outbreaks can defoliate trees.

fm.Color: Tan-brown; larvae build silk tents in trees

desc: Clio tiger moth — striking black-and-white tiger moth. Larvae ('woolly bears') on various herbs. Fast erratic flight when disturbed. Desert and foothill species.

fm.Color: Bold black and white; fast flier

desc: Triangular Anopina. Tortricid moth. Larvae roll leaves.
Edwards' graphic moth — gray erebid with distinctive line pattern. Named for lepidopterist Henry Edwards. Foothill chaparral.
Packard's geometrid — small gray geometrid of chaparral and scrub. Named for entomologist A.S. Packard. Common at lights.
Affiliated stamnodes — small pale geometrid of chaparral. Related to other Stamnodes species. Larvae are inchworms on native shrubs. At lights.
Toyon leaf miner moth — microscopic nepticulid whose larvae create serpentine mines in toyon (Heteromeles) leaves. Mines visible as pale winding trails.
Dark-bordered Granite. Geometrid moth. Larvae are loopers.
Straight-banded wave — small geometrid with straight dark crossband. On native shrubs in foothill habitats. Inchworm larvae.
New Mexico Carpet Moth. Geometrid moth. Larvae are loopers.
European pepper moth — invasive crambid from Mediterranean. Serious greenhouse pest on many crops including peppers, tomatoes, and ornamentals. Expanding in SoCal.
Light-spotted Quaker. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Brown ctenucha — brown day-flying erebid moth with orange head. Related to the more common Virginia ctenucha. On flowers in foothill habitats.
Soybean Looper Moth. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Joined underwing — gray bark-patterned forewings concealing bright banded hindwings. Classic flash-and-vanish display when startled from tree trunks. Larvae on willow.

fm.Color: Gray bark FW; bright banded HW

desc: Girdler Moth. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Scarlet flower moth — small noctuid colored to match its host flower. Rests inside and feeds on flower heads. Color and pattern match specific blooms.
Arizona lappet moth — medium brown lasiocampid. Larvae densely hairy. Feeds on various trees and shrubs. Mountain and foothill canyons.
Long-sac nepticulid — one of the smallest moths — wingspan under 5mm. Larvae mine in leaves creating characteristic serpentine trails.
Ilia underwing — large gray moth with spectacular orange-red hindwings with black bands. Bark-mimicking forewings conceal bright color. Flash display in flight. Larvae on oaks.

fm.Color: Gray bark FW; orange-red + black banded HW

desc: Epict geometrid — small geometrid of foothill habitats. Inchworm larvae on various plants. At lights. Part of the species-rich geometrid fauna.
Early casebearer — small tineoid moth similar to household casebearer. Larvae build portable silk cases on walls and ceilings. Feeds on detritus.

fm.Color: Gray; portable silk cases on walls

desc: American lappet moth — brown lasiocampid with scalloped wing margins mimicking dead leaf cluster. Larvae on willow and poplar. Cryptic.

fm.Color: Brown; scalloped wings mimic dead leaves

desc: Sub-apical pug moth — tiny geometrid. Part of the enormous Eupithecia genus — 'pugs' are notoriously difficult to identify. Larvae on various flowers.
Marine green noctuid — small olive-green noctuid. Coastal habitats. Larvae on sea-fig (Carpobrotus) and other ice plants. Uncommon.
Silver-spotted tiger moth — white tiger moth with metallic silver spots and orange-yellow thorax. Spectacular. Woolly bear larvae on Douglas-fir and other conifers.

fm.Color: White with silver spots; orange thorax — spectacular

desc: Ello sphinx — large brown sphinx moth. Larvae feed on euphorbs and cassava. Occasional migrant to SoCal from tropical regions. Very fast flier.
Bougainvillea Caterpillar Moth. Crambid moth.
Large batrachedra — small narrow-winged moth. Larvae mine in palm fronds. Can damage ornamental palms. Common wherever palms grow in LA.
Reclusive dart — gray-brown noctuid. Cutworm larvae feed at soil surface at night, cutting seedlings. Part of the large dart moth genus Euxoa.
Elcodes moth — small crambid of tropical affinity. Larvae on various plants. Occasional in SoCal from southern populations.
Western Avocado Leafroller Moth. Tortricid moth. Larvae roll leaves.
American Dun-bar Moth. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Curved-lined Angle. Geometrid moth. Larvae are loopers.
Olive crambid — small olive-brown crambid. Larvae on various plants. Common at lights in foothill habitats.
Lantana Stick Moth. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Two-lined wave — small pale geometrid with two fine dark crosslines. Inchworm larvae on native shrubs. At lights in foothill habitats.

fm.Color: Pale with two fine dark lines

desc: Ypsolopha moth — small narrow-winged moth. Larvae on various trees and shrubs. Part of the diverse micro-moth fauna. Upturned wing tips at rest.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; upturned wing tips at rest

desc: Sweet stilbosis — tiny cosmet moth. Larvae mine in plant tissues. One of many overlooked micro-moths in the cosmopterigid family.
Bluegrass webworm moth — small straw-colored crambid. Larvae web and feed on bluegrass and other turf grasses. Can damage lawns. Common at lights near turf.
Minute heliozelid — one of the smallest moths — wingspan about 4mm. Metallic scales. Larvae mine in leaves. Shield-bearer moth family.
Gray furcula — pale notodontid with distinctive forked hindwing fringe. Larvae on willow and poplar. Caterpillars have remarkable defensive posture — forked tail.

fm.Color: Pale gray; larvae with forked tail

desc: Feast pyrausta — small orange-and-brown crambid. Day-flying. On mint family plants in sage scrub. Similar to other Pyrausta species.
Tricolored Acrobasis Moth. Pyralid moth.
Distinct tolype — gray lasiocampid with bark-like pattern. Furry. Larvae on various trees in mountain habitats. Tented wings give distinctive profile at rest.
Civil Rustic Moth. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Warming gelechiid — tiny moth. Larvae mine in leaves. Part of the enormous micro-moth fauna that dominates blacklight samples by numbers.
Lorquin's Angle. Geometrid moth. Larvae are loopers.
Scribbled Sallow. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Four-spotted yellowneck — small moth with four dark spots on tawny wings. Larvae in leaf litter and detritus. Originally Mediterranean, now widespread.
Spring sun moth — tiny metallic day-flying moth. Bright metallic scales reflect sunlight. Larvae on various plants. Beautiful under magnification.
Southern angle moth — small pale geometrid with angular wing markings. Inchworm larvae on various trees. At lights. Common across SoCal.
Wood-colored geometrid — medium geometrid colored like dead wood. Cryptic. Inchworm larvae on various trees and shrubs. At lights in foothill habitats.
Sharp-winged pug — tiny geometrid with pointed wing tips. Part of the difficult Eupithecia genus. Larvae feed inside flowers and flower buds.
Sharp-stigma Looper Moth. Noctuid moth. Attracted to lights.
Winter quaker — gray-brown noctuid flying in winter months. One of the few moths active during coldest season. At bait traps and lights. Larvae on oaks.
Florida pink scavenger — tiny cosmet moth feeding on dried plant matter, insect debris, and detritus. Found in stored products. Pantropical.
Similar noctuid — gray-brown noctuid of foothill habitats. Larvae on various plants. At lights. Part of the very diverse western noctuid fauna.
Cotton stem moth — tiny gelechiid whose larvae bore into cotton stems and pink bollworm-damaged bolls. Pest of cotton. Also on other malvaceous plants.
Braun's nepticulid — microscopic leaf-mining moth. One of the smallest moths in North America. Mines in rose family leaves.
Narrow-barred pigmy moth — microscopic nepticulid mining in rose leaves. Mines are narrow serpentine tracks. Common wherever roses grow.

fm.Color: Metallic; serpentine mines in rose leaves

desc: Beautiful-winged flower moth — small colorful noctuid visiting and matching specific flower hosts. Larvae feed inside flower heads. Color matches host flowers.
Date-marked wave — small delicate geometrid with circular wing discal marks. Larvae on oaks. Common at lights in foothill woodland.
Bird nest moth — small tineoid moth whose larvae feed in bird nests on feathers, hair, and detritus. Also in wasp nests. Important decomposer of keratin.

fm.Color: Brown; in bird and wasp nests

desc: White-tipped stamnodes — small geometrid of foothill chaparral. Pale with white-tipped wings. Larvae on native shrubs. At lights.
Marked symmoca — tiny autostichid moth. Larvae in detritus. Common at lights but overlooked due to tiny size. Part of the vast micro-moth diversity.
Chalky bird-dropping moth — tiny noctuid perfectly mimicking a fresh bird dropping. White and gray splotch pattern. Remarkably convincing camouflage on leaves.
Bean emerald — small green geometrid. Larvae camouflage themselves with flower petals glued to body. Fades from green to white after death.

fm.Color: Emerald green; larvae wear flower petal camouflage

desc: Astrologer moth — tiny noctuid of early spring. One of the first moths to fly each year. Larvae on spring wildflowers including baby blue eyes.
Sinuous geometrid — large gray geometrid with wavy crosslines. Related to the famous peppered moth (B. betularia). Larvae on various trees.
Gem moth — small delicate geometrid with intricate pattern. Migratory — can appear anywhere. Larvae on many low plants. Very widespread.
Madrone skin miner — tiny moth whose larvae create winding mines in madrone and manzanita bark. White serpentine trails on smooth red bark are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Pale; serpentine bark mines on madrone

desc: Montana six-plume moth — bizarre moth with each wing divided into six separate feather-like plumes. 24 total plumes. One of the most unusual-looking moths. Tiny.

fm.Color: Pale; each wing split into 6 feathered plumes — 24 total

desc: Hooked geometrid — geometrid moth with distinctively hooked/falcate wing tips. Common in foothill chaparral. Larvae on buckwheat.
Four-lined arches — small gray noctuid with four dark lines on forewing. Common at lights in foothill habitats. Larvae on low plants.

fm.Color: Gray with four dark parallel lines

desc: Veil flower moth — small noctuid resting inside and matching flowers. Larvae develop in flower heads. Color perfectly matches host plant blooms.
Most beautiful emerald — small bright green geometrid. As the name suggests, one of the most attractive emerald moths. Larvae on oaks in chaparral.
Six-signed litocala — dark erebid with six pale marks on wings. Desert and foothill scrub. Larvae on creosote bush and other shrubs.
Morena gelechiid — tiny dark gelechiid moth. Larvae mine in plant stems. Part of the vast but poorly known micro-moth fauna of California chaparral.
Red-lined moth — small noctuid with distinctive red-orange lines on gray wings. One of the more recognizable small noctuids. Day-flying in desert scrub.
Ring-marked stamnodes — small geometrid with distinctive ring-shaped discal mark. Chaparral and sage scrub. At lights.
Phaeton primrose sphinx — spectacular day-flying sphinx moth with pink hindwings. Visits flowers at dawn/dusk. Larvae on evening primrose. Desert species. Rare.
Flame fairy moth — tiny metallic moth with extremely long antennae (♂ several times body length). Day-flying in spring. Larvae in leaf litter. Beautiful under magnification.
Large paectes — medium gray-brown noctuid with scalloped wing edges. Larvae on sumac and cashew family plants. Cryptic bark-resting posture.
Stored grain moth — small gray-brown pyralid in stored products. Larvae feed on grain, dried foods, and detritus. Common in pantries and warehouses.
Morning-glory leafminer — tiny moth whose larvae create blotch mines in morning glory leaves. Distinctive clear window-pane mines. Very common on bindweed.

fm.Color: Pale; blotch mines on morning glory

desc: Sulphur moth — bright yellow geometrid with brown speckling. Striking color. Larvae on ceanothus and other chaparral shrubs. One of the most colorful geometrids.

fm.Color: Bright sulphur yellow with brown speckles

desc: Bordered fawn moth — fawn-brown geometrid with darker border. Larvae on various trees. Common at lights in foothill woodland.
Double-banded carpet moth — geometrid with two prominent dark crossbands on pale wings. On various trees and shrubs. Common at lights.

fm.Color: Pale with two bold dark bands

desc: Western clothes moth — tiny tineoid moth. Larvae feed on wool, fur, feathers, and other animal fibers. Pest of stored textiles. Makes portable case.
Strawberry crown moth — clearwing moth with transparent wings mimicking a wasp. Larvae bore in strawberry crowns. Day-flying. Can damage strawberry plantings.
Triplex cutworm moth — small gray noctuid. Larvae are cutworms feeding at soil level. On various low plants.
Emerald geometrid — small bright green geometrid. Like other emeralds, fades to white after death. Larvae on various plants.

fm.Color: Bright emerald green; fades after death

desc: Dale pyrausta — small orange-yellow crambid with dark markings. On mint family plants. Similar to P. unifascialis.
Shell-shaped noctuid — small distinctive noctuid with shell-like wing shape at rest. Rare California endemic. Foothill chaparral.
Unequal-lined geometrid — small pale geometrid with uneven crosslines. Larvae on various shrubs. Common at lights.
Sunflower bud moth — tiny gelechiid whose larvae bore into sunflower buds and stems. Can reduce sunflower seed production. Common where sunflowers grow.
Tabby moth — small warm-brown pyralid with orange-pink tinge. Larvae in stored hay and dried vegetation. Common at lights near agricultural areas.
Bear tiger moth — black forewings with cream stripes and red-orange hindwings with black spots. Woolly bear caterpillars. Less common than A. proxima in LA foothills.

fm.Color: Black FW + cream stripes; red-orange HW

desc: Withered geometrid — small pale geometrid with delicate crosslines. Common at lights. Larvae on various shrubs.
White-headed mompha — tiny moth with distinctive white head contrasting with darker wings. Larvae mine in evening primrose (Oenothera) stems and seed pods.

fm.Color: Dark wings; white head; mines evening primrose

desc: Fine-lined sallow — gray noctuid with fine pale streaks on forewing. Common at lights in spring. Larvae on various low plants.
Sooty-winged chalcoela — distinctive crambid with dark sooty wings and bright orange thorax. Larvae parasitize paper wasp nests, eating wasp larvae.

fm.Color: Dark sooty wings; bright orange thorax
fm.Behavior: Larvae parasitize paper wasp nests

desc: Citrus cutworm moth — brown-gray noctuid. Larvae (cutworms) damage citrus and other crops by feeding on buds and leaves. Winter-flying adult.
Merry graphic moth — brown erebid with wavy crosslines. Larvae on mesquite and acacia. Desert and foothill species. At lights.
Fawn geometrid — large pale brown geometrid. Larvae on willows and other trees. Common at lights in riparian areas.

fm.Color: Pale fawn brown; large for a geometrid

desc: Soybean webworm moth — tiny gelechiid. Larvae web leaves of legumes. Minor pest. At lights.
Walnut spanworm moth — large gray geometrid. Inchworm larvae are significant walnut pests — can defoliate trees. Males fly in winter; females flightless.
Red-stoned quaker — gray-brown noctuid with reddish reniform spot. Spring-flying. Larvae on various trees. Common at bait traps.
Brown-shouldered henricus — medium tortricid with dark shoulder patch. Larvae roll leaves of various trees. Foothill woodland.
Carmel cochylid — small tortricid named for Carmel, CA. On native Asteraceae. Larvae in flower heads. Chaparral.
Singular noctuid — small gray noctuid of desert and foothill habitats. Larvae on various plants.
Obscure sphinx — large dark sphinx moth. Fast powerful flier visiting flowers at dusk. Larvae on euphorbs. Occasional visitor from tropics.
Pale lichen moth — tiny white-and-orange lichen moth. Larvae feed on lichens growing on bark and rocks. Delicate. Common at lights.
Sea-heath plume moth — unusual plume moth with undivided (not feathered) wings — unique among plume moths. On sea-heath and other coastal plants.

fm.Color: Gray; unusually undivided wings for plume moth

desc: Monica's geometrid — small pale geometrid of coastal areas. Named for Santa Monica. Larvae on native shrubs.
Spurred wave moth — small geometrid with single spur mark on wing. Foothill chaparral. At lights.
California grass-veneer — small crambid on grasses. One of several grass-veneer moths in the pyraloid complex. At lights in grassland areas.
Behrens' quaker — gray-brown noctuid. Spring-flying. Named for California naturalist James Behrens. Larvae on oaks and other trees.
Pacific quaker — gray-brown noctuid of spring. Common at bait traps and lights. Larvae on various trees. Part of the early spring moth fauna.
October thorn moth — large pale geometrid flying in fall. Named for October flight period. Larvae on various trees. One of the few fall-specialist moths.

fm.Color: Pale tan; flies in fall — unusual

desc: Chrysanthemum flower borer — tiny tortricid whose larvae bore into chrysanthemum flower heads. Pest of ornamental chrysanthemums.
Smoky graphic moth — dark smoky gray erebid. Desert and foothill habitats. At lights. Larvae on shrubs.
White annaphila — tiny noctuid of early spring with white wing patches. Day-flying in wildflower fields. Larvae on baby blue eyes (Nemophila).

fm.Color: Dark with white patches; day-flying in spring

desc: Perpendicular graphic — brown erebid with sharp-angled crosslines. On legumes. Desert and foothill habitat. At lights.
Freed-spot lichen moth — tiny orange-and-black lichen moth. Larvae eat lichens on rocks and bark. Delicate and colorful.
Fly-specked geometrid — pale geometrid peppered with tiny dark specks like fly spots. On various shrubs. At lights.
Poison hemlock moth — brown depressariid whose larvae feed on poison hemlock (Conium). Potential biocontrol agent for invasive hemlock. Day-active.
Crucial quaker — gray-brown noctuid with cruciform stigma mark. Spring-flying. Common at bait traps in foothill woodland.
Anvil-wing moth — dark erebid with anvil-shaped wing mark. At lights. Larvae on low plants. Distinctive mark diagnostic.
White-striped oecophorid — tiny moth with white streak on dark wings. On tree bark. Larvae in bark crevice detritus.
Half-red pyrausta — small crambid with reddish-orange and brown wings. On mint family plants. Day-flying in sage scrub.
Cleopatra underwing — gray bark-patterned FW concealing bright yellow-orange HW. Classic flash-and-vanish display. Larvae on willow.
Stigma moth — tiny gelechiid with distinctive stigma mark. On native shrubs. Part of the vast but poorly known micro-moth fauna.
Beautiful-scaled hooded moth — gray noctuid with hooded thorax. Larvae on Asteraceae. Distinctive dagger-like wing shape at rest.

fm.Color: Gray; hooded thorax; dagger shape at rest

desc: Pacific anhimella — small gray noctuid. Larvae on various plants. Part of the diverse western noctuid fauna.
Blue-margined emerald — bright green geometrid with bluish-white wing margins. One of several beautiful emerald moths. Larvae on oaks.
Orange-banded jewel sun moth — spectacular tiny day-flying moth with metallic scales. Larvae mine madrone and manzanita leaves. Among the most beautiful micro-moths.
Ringed stamnodes — small geometrid with ring-shaped markings on wings. Chaparral. At lights.
Twelve-lined crambid — pale crambid with twelve fine parallel lines on wings. Distinctive. At lights.
London plane leafminer — tiny moth mining blotch mines in plane tree leaves. Introduced from Europe. Common on London plane trees in LA streets.

fm.Color: Golden with white streaks; mines plane tree leaves

desc: Black-banded carpet moth — medium geometrid with bold dark crossband. On various herbaceous plants. At lights.
V-lined quaker — gray noctuid with V-shaped crosslines. Common at lights in spring. Larvae on oaks and other trees.
Sub-white geometrid — small pale geometrid. At lights in desert and foothill habitats. Larvae on various plants.
Indeterminate dart — gray-brown noctuid. Larvae are cutworms. At lights. Part of the cutworm moth complex.
Beautiful cosmopterix — tiny moth with striking metallic scales and orange bands. One of the most beautiful micro-moths when viewed under magnification. Leaf miner.

fm.Color: Metallic with orange bands — stunning under magnification

desc: Zigzag furcula — gray notodontid with zigzag crosslines. Larvae have forked tail ('furcula'). On willows and poplars. At lights.

fm.Color: Gray with zigzag lines; forked-tail larvae

desc: Bold euclidia — day-flying erebid with contrasting black-and-white wing pattern. On clover and alfalfa fields. Unusual for an erebid in being diurnal.
Dark-collared tinea — tiny clothes moth relative. Larvae on dried animal material — feathers, hair, wool. Can damage textiles.
Weasel-like crambid — small brown crambid with fine crosslines. At lights. Larvae on various herbaceous plants.
Tinted mouralia — medium dark noctuid with subtle coloring. Foothill and mountain woodland. At lights.
False celery leaftier — small crambid whose larvae web together leaves of celery and related plants. Minor pest.
White-banded geometrid — green-brown geometrid with prominent white crossband. Larvae on willows and other trees. Mountain species.
Darkened zenophleps — medium dark geometrid. Mountain and foothill woodland. At lights. Larvae on various trees.
MacDunnough's pero — large brown geometrid. Distinctive angular wing shape. At lights in oak woodland. Larvae on various trees.
California tissue moth — medium geometrid. Found hibernating in caves and mines. One of few cave-resting moths. Larvae on buckthorn.
Indiscrete cissusa — brown-gray erebid with intricate pattern. Cryptic on bark. Common at lights in foothill woodland.
Single fairy moth — tiny metallic moth with extremely long antennae (♂ 3× body length). Day-flying swarms in spring. Larvae in leaf litter. Beautiful.
Electalis pyralid — small brown pyralid of stored products and detritus. In old buildings. Larvae feed on waste organic material.
Creosote moth — medium geometrid associated with creosote bush (Larrea). Desert species. Larvae feed on creosote. At lights.
Lamiaria oecophorid — small oecophorid. Larvae on dead plant material. At lights.
Bronzed yucca moth — small moth associated with yucca. Unlike its relative the yucca moth (Tegeticula), Prodoxus larvae bore in yucca stems rather than pollinating flowers.
Unconquered needle miner — tiny gelechiid whose larvae mine inside conifer needles. Can damage pine and fir foliage.
Oak ribbed casemaker — tiny moth whose larvae mine then skeletonize oak leaves, creating ribbed cocoons. White ribbed cocoons on bark are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Pale; white ribbed cocoons on oak bark

desc: Mountain mahogany shield-bearer — tiny heliozelid mining mountain mahogany leaves. Cuts shield-shaped larval case from leaf. Chaparral.

fm.Color: Metallic; shield-shaped cases on mountain mahogany

desc: Willow shield-bearer — tiny heliozelid mining willow leaves. Shield-shaped cocoon cut from leaf tissue. Common on willows.

fm.Color: Metallic; shield-shaped cases on willow leaves

desc: Schaeffer's aquatic moth — remarkable crambid whose larvae are fully aquatic, living in silk cases on rocks in streams. Larvae have gills. One of few aquatic moths.

fm.Note: Larvae fully aquatic with gills — remarkable

desc: Citrus peelminer — tiny moth whose larvae mine serpentine trails in citrus fruit rind. Cosmetic damage to fruit. Important citrus pest in California.

fm.Color: Pale; serpentine mines on citrus rind

desc: Rivulet nocloa — small gray noctuid with rivulet-like wavy crosslines. Desert and scrub. At lights.
Rose leaf miner — extremely tiny moth mining serpentine trails in rose leaves. White winding trails on rose leaves diagnostic. Wingspan ~5mm.

fm.Color: Dark; serpentine mines on rose leaves

desc: Russian thistle casebearer — tiny moth whose larvae build portable cases from plant material. On Russian thistle (tumbleweed). Potential biocontrol.

fm.Color: Gray; portable larval case; on tumbleweed

desc: Coffeeberry midrib gall moth — tiny moth inducing galls along the midrib of coffeeberry (Frangula) leaves. Galls cause leaf distortion. Host-specific.
White-thorax nepticulid — extremely tiny moth (among the world's smallest). Leaf miner on various plants. Wingspan barely 4mm.

fm.WS: ¼ in; among world's smallest moths

desc: Ridings' forester — striking black-and-white day-flying noctuid with orange collar. Mimics distasteful insects. On evening primrose. Beautiful.
Giant sphinx — one of the largest moths in North America. Massive gray sphinx with 5+ inch wingspan. Rare vagrant from tropics. Larvae on Annonaceae.

fm.Note: Among largest N. American moths — rare vagrant

desc: Ceanothus stem gall moth — tiny cosmopterigid whose larvae induce stem galls on ceanothus (California lilac). Galls are swollen stem sections.
Deduced graphic moth — small gray noctuid with fine dark markings. Desert and foothill scrub. At lights.
Western underwing — large gray bark-patterned moth concealing colorful hindwings. Classic flash-and-vanish display. Larvae on willow. The quintessential underwing moth.

fm.Color: Gray bark FW; colored HW; flash display

desc: Verrill's underwing — gray underwing moth with banded hindwings. Flash display when startled. Larvae on oak. Named for zoologist A.E. Verrill.
Speyer's hooded owlet — gray noctuid with hooded thorax that gives distinctive silhouette at rest. Larvae on goldenrod and related composites.
Spotted datana — brown notodontid with dark streaks. Larvae are social caterpillars that rest in head-down clusters on branches. On various trees.

fm.Behavior: Larvae cluster head-down on branches

desc: Ragweed borer moth — small tortricid boring in ragweed stems. Potential biocontrol of invasive ragweed. Introduced deliberately in some regions.
Red-bordered wave — tiny wave moth with reddish wing border. One of several Idaea wave moths. At lights.
Mousy plume moth — tiny pale plume moth with mouse-gray coloring. Deeply divided feathery wings. On various plants.
Woodgrain rivulet — small geometrid with fine parallel lines resembling woodgrain. On Indian paintbrush (Castilleja). Distinctive pattern.
Frothy moth — gray noctuid. Named for frothy/spumous appearance of wing pattern. Larvae on cocklebur and related composites.
California plataea — medium brown geometrid endemic to California. Chaparral and woodland. At lights.
Arizona bird-dropping moth — tiny noctuid mimicking a bird dropping on a leaf. White-and-brown splotch pattern. Remarkably convincing camouflage. Larvae on ragweed.
Scissored arches — medium geometrid with forked/scissored wing tips. Distinctive wing shape. On various shrubs. At lights.
Sharp-lined yellow — medium bright yellow geometrid with sharp dark lines. One of the more colorful geometrids. At lights. Larvae on many trees.

fm.Color: Bright yellow with sharp dark lines

desc: Busck's gallmoth — small tortricid. Named for entomologist A. Busck. Larvae may induce galls on native plants.
Graef's flower moth — small colorful noctuid on native composites. Color matches host flowers. Part of the flower-matching Schinia guild.
Nefrax oecophorid — small brown concealer moth. Larvae on dead plant material. Part of the oecophorid detritivore guild.
Pistachio emerald — bright green geometrid. Like other emeralds, fades after death. Larvae on various trees including pistachio.
Two-formed graphic — gray erebid with variable wing pattern (biformata = two forms). Desert and foothill scrub. At lights.
Radiating pero — large geometrid with radiating wing lines. At lights in foothill woodland.
Kearfott's tubeworm moth — small brown acrolophid. Larvae build silk tubes in soil/thatch. On grass roots. Part of a poorly known moth family.
California leucogoniella — tiny pale gelechiid. On native plants. Part of California's poorly known micro-moth fauna.
Paraplutella gelechiid — tiny gray moth. Larvae in plant stems or leaf webs. Part of the vast micro-moth fauna.
Castle oecophorid — small oecophorid on native vegetation. Larvae likely on dead plant material.
Masked plataea — medium geometrid with mask-like dark markings. Foothill and mountain woodland.
Brown woodling — brown spring-flying noctuid. Common at bait traps. Larvae on various trees. One of the first spring moths.
Powell's embola — small gray-brown erebid. Named for lepidopterist Jerry Powell. Chaparral and foothill habitats. At lights.
Tessellated stamnodes — small geometrid with checkerboard-like markings. Chaparral. At lights.
Lesser wax moth — small gray moth infesting bee hives. Larvae eat wax comb. Pest of apiaries. Males produce ultrasonic courtship songs.

fm.Damage: Bee hive pest — eats wax comb

desc: White-trimmed abegesta — small pyralid with white wing borders. At lights.
Single-banded aroga — tiny dark gelechiid with single pale band. Larvae in plant stems.
Streaked twirler — tiny gelechiid with streaked wing pattern. Larvae in nightshade family plants (Solanaceae). Can mine potato stems.
Small pale crambid of chaparral. At lights. Larvae on various plants.
Othello dagger moth — gray noctuid with dagger-shaped markings. Larvae with striking hair tufts. On various trees. Named for Shakespeare's character.
Twisted phobolosia — small gray noctuid with twisted/sinuous crosslines. Chaparral and scrub. At lights.
Buttercup golden hair — small golden-yellow noctuid. Larvae on buttercup (Ranunculus) and other flowers. Spring-flying. Attractive.
Ovate gracillariid — tiny moth whose larvae mine then fold leaves. Complex multi-instar larval behavior — starts mining, then becomes leaf-folder.
Clemente's quaker — gray-brown noctuid. Named for San Clemente Island. Foothill and coastal habitats. At lights.
Margined yucca moth — small moth boring in yucca fruit stalks. Part of the yucca-moth complex but a parasite rather than pollinator.

fm.Color: Pale with dark margins; in yucca

desc: MacDunnough's geometrid — medium moth named for lepidopterist J. McDunnough. Chaparral and scrub habitats. At lights.
Chemsak's geometrid — small geometrid named for entomologist J.A. Chemsak. Desert and foothill scrub. At lights.
Western Joshua tree moth — the obligate pollinator of western Joshua trees. Famous mutualism — only insect that pollinates Joshua tree. Larvae eat some seeds as payment.

fm.Note: Sole pollinator of Joshua tree — iconic mutualism

desc: Little royal — tiny black-and-orange lichen moth. Day-flying. Resembles a Lycid beetle — toxic mimic. Larvae eat lichens.

fm.Color: Black and orange; day-flying beetle mimic

desc: Ultra parexcelsa — small noctuid of desert and foothill habitats. At lights.
Phacelia noctuid — small moth whose larvae feed on phacelia wildflowers. On native wildflowers in chaparral.
Ceanothus gelechiid — tiny moth whose larvae feed on ceanothus (California lilac). Host-specific native moth on a native shrub.
Benign borer — small tortricid whose larvae bore in plant stems. On composites. At lights.
Glossy black idia — small dark glossy erebid. Under bark and in tree hollows. Larvae eat dead leaves and detritus. Common at bait traps.
Clark's sphinx — medium brown sphinx moth. Larvae on evening primrose (Clarkia). Day-flying. Hovers at flowers like a hummingbird.
Erect tornos — small angular geometrid. Desert and scrub habitats. At lights.
Purple agrochola — warm brown noctuid with purplish tinge. Fall-flying. Larvae on herbaceous plants. At bait traps.
Pink-washed aristotelia — tiny pinkish gelechiid. Larvae mine or web leaves of various plants. Delicate pink coloring.
Shaded granite moth — small geometrid with gently curved crosslines. On various plants. At lights in foothill areas.
Outcurved geometrid — small geometrid with outcurved crosslines. Chaparral and scrub. At lights.
Placid pug moth — tiny geometrid. Part of the enormous genus Eupithecia (pugs) — the most species-rich moth genus. At lights.
Western wave — tiny pale wave moth. Very common at lights but easily overlooked. Larvae on low plants.
Western idia — small dark erebid. Under bark and in leaf litter. Larvae on dead leaves. At bait traps.
Ceanothus nola moth — tiny gray nolid associated with ceanothus. Tufted wing scales. One of the more obscure micro-moth families.
Transparent quaker — pale spring-flying noctuid with somewhat translucent wings. At bait traps.
Morrison's pero — large gray-brown geometrid. Named for H.K. Morrison. Larvae on various trees. At lights.
Crossed golden arches — colorful noctuid with golden-orange and dark markings. On composites in desert scrub. Day-flying. Attractive.
Tissue moth — similar to T. californiata. Hibernates in caves and cool sheltered spots. Papery/tissue-like wing texture.
Sweet clover root borer — tiny cosmopterigid whose larvae bore into sweet clover roots. Potential biocontrol of invasive sweet clover. Named for entomologist B.D. Walsh.
Lesser bean looper — green-gold noctuid. Larvae (semi-loopers) on beans and various crops. At lights.
Buta flower moth — small noctuid resting inside flower heads matching host flower color. Larvae in flower heads.
Ligea's flower moth — small flower-matching noctuid. Rests inside flowers by day. Larvae develop inside flower heads.
Mountain mahogany looper — gray geometrid whose inchworm larvae defoliate mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus). Important in chaparral ecology.
Dark acrobasis — small gray-brown pyralid. Larvae on oaks and other trees. At lights.
Green-tinged bryolymnia — small gray-green noctuid. Larvae on various plants. Uncommon.
Greeting stegea — small pyralid. At lights in chaparral and foothill habitats.
Stenomid oak leaf tier — tiny moth whose larvae tie oak leaves together with silk. On various oaks. Part of the diverse oak-feeding moth community.

fm.Color: Brown; ties oak leaves with silk

desc: Desert lichen moth — tiny lichen moth of desert habitats. Larvae eat lichens on rocks. Delicate.
Scale-feeding snout moth — small pyralid whose larvae eat scale insects. Important natural enemy. One of few Lepidoptera that are predatory.

fm.Note: Predatory larvae eat scale insects — unusual for moths

desc: Falcate geometrid — medium geometrid with sickle-shaped (falcate) wing tips. Desert and foothill habitats. At lights.
Decia annaphila — tiny spring-flying noctuid. Day-active in wildflower meadows. Larvae on native wildflowers. Uncommon.
Straw-colored dasyblemma — tiny pale tineoid moth. Larvae on dead plant material. Part of the detritivore micro-moth guild.
Hutson's ponometia — tiny noctuid similar to bird-dropping moths. On composites. At lights.
Cervella ypsolophid — small pale moth with upturned wing tips. On various trees. Part of the diverse western micro-moth fauna.
Red-tinged slossonia — small noctuid with reddish coloring. Uncommon. Desert and foothill habitats.
Cactus crambid — small brown crambid associated with prickly pear cactus. Larvae feed in cactus detritus and under pads.
Black-and-white epinotia — tiny tortricid with contrasting dark and pale pattern. On various trees. At lights.
Tepper's noctuid — gray moth named for early California collector Tepper. On composites. At lights.
Third perigonica — small gray noctuid. At lights. Part of the diverse western noctuid fauna.
Pallid geometrid — small pale geometrid. At lights in foothill and desert habitats.
Gold-blue archaic moth — extremely primitive moth (Eriocraniidae) with metallic gold-blue scales. Mines oak leaves. Among the most ancient moth lineages still living.
Implicated geometrid — medium geometrid of woodland habitats. Larvae on various herbaceous plants. At lights.
Neumoegen's golden hair — small golden-yellow noctuid with dark marks. Named for collector B. Neumoegen. On composites in chaparral.
White-angled epinotia — tiny tortricid with white wing angle mark. On various trees. At lights.
Marga cossid — medium carpenter moth. Larvae bore in tree trunks and roots — can take 2-3 years to develop. Thick-bodied. At lights.
California dejongia — tiny oecophorid. On native plants. Part of California's poorly known micro-moth fauna.
Medusa moth — large lasiocampid (lappet moth). Larvae with dense hair tufts. On oaks and other trees. Named for Medusa's snake-like hair.
Single-banded archaic moth — extremely primitive moth from ancient lineage (Acanthopteroctetidae). Tiny. Metallic. California endemic family. Among the most primitive Lepidoptera.

fm.Note: Ancient lineage — among most primitive moths

desc: Splendid tischeriid — tiny leaf-mining moth. Larvae make blotch mines in leaves. Metallic coloring visible only under magnification.
Ragan's noctuid — small gray moth. Part of the diverse western noctuid fauna. At lights.
Two-banded archaic moth — another extremely primitive eriocraniid moth. Leaf miner on oaks. Ancient lineage predating most other Lepidoptera families.
Encelia bucculatrix — tiny moth whose larvae mine encelia (brittlebush) leaves in coastal sage scrub. Host-specific.
Jewel-studded four o'clock sun moth — spectacular tiny day-flying heliozelid with metallic scales. Mines four o'clock (Mirabilis) leaves. Among the most beautiful micro-moths.

fm.Color: Metallic jewel-like; day-flying; on four o'clock plants

desc: Truncated lipographis — tiny dark tortricid. On native trees and shrubs. Part of the vast tortricid diversity.
Divine annaphila — tiny noctuid of early spring. Day-flying among wildflowers. Part of the specialized spring-flying noctuid guild.
One-spotted crambid — tiny pale crambid with single dark spot. On grasses. At lights.
Six-spotted noctuid — small gray moth with six dark spots. On composites. At lights.
Elsinore white moth — white geometrid with subtle markings. One of several white geometrids. At lights.
Field's tallula — small pyralid. Named for collector Field. At lights.
Five-spotted twirler — tiny gelechiid with five dark spots. Larvae feed inside plant stems or seed heads.
Snakeweed borer moth — small tortricid boring in snakeweed (Gutierrezia) stems. Host-specific. Foothill and desert scrub.
Walter's silkmoth — medium saturnid. California native. Larvae on ceanothus. One of the smaller silkmoths. Named for its discoverers.
Guenée's geometrid — small pale geometrid with dark crosslines. Named for French entomologist A. Guenée. At lights.
Pacific clearwing — small sesiid with transparent wings mimicking a wasp. Larvae bore in shrub stems. Day-flying wasp mimic.
